description: Quality rules for chess, checkers, tic-tac-toe, and other board/grid games.

guidelines.rule: The human player clicks/taps to make moves. AI opponent moves automatically AFTER the human. Never auto-play both sides.
guidelines.why: Same as card games — the #1 bug is auto-playing both players.
examples: Human clicks a square to place their piece
AI responds after a brief delay (300-500ms)

guidelines.rule: Validate all moves before allowing them. Don't let players make illegal moves (e.g., moving into check in chess, playing on occupied squares).
guidelines.why: Invalid moves break game state and confuse users.
examples: Highlight valid move squares on hover/select
Show error message for invalid moves

guidelines.rule: Detect win/draw conditions automatically and show a clear game-over state with the result and a 'Play Again' button.
guidelines.why: Games that don't detect when someone won leave the user confused about whether the game is still going.
examples: Check win after every move
Show 'You Win!' / 'AI Wins' / 'Draw' overlay
Highlight winning line/pieces

guidelines.rule: Use an alternating-color grid (light/dark squares) for board games. Make the grid clearly defined with borders.
guidelines.why: A plain white grid with thin lines is hard to read. Alternating colors are the universal board game visual.
examples: Chess: #f0d9b5 light, #b58863 dark
Go: wood-colored board with black lines

guidelines.rule: If the game has a human player, there MUST be at least one AI/computer opponent to play against. Never generate a game where the user has to play both sides manually. For web mini-apps using Lego components, mount one of the provided AI components — `entities/ChessAI` for chess, `entities/CheckersAI` for checkers, or `entities/SimpleOpponent` (random legal moves) as the universal fallback for any TurnBasedManager-driven game. After the human moves, schedule the AI's reply via `setTimeout(() => { const m = ai.pickMove(board, aiColor); if (m) applyMove(m); }, 350)` — the small delay sells 'thinking' and gives the human time to register their own move. ChessAI default depth is 2; raise to 3 for stronger play. CheckersAI default depth is 3.
guidelines.why: Users ask for a 'playable' game expecting someone to play against. A board with no opponent forces the user to move pieces for both sides, which is not a game — it's a demo. Until 2026-05-02 our chess/checkers golden assemblies HAD this bug — the user moved white and the board froze waiting for them to also move black. Adding the AI components and this rule together closes the loop. The marker check `requiresAIOpponent` now refuses to publish a 2-player game without one of the registered AI components OR an inline `selectComputerMove`/`cpuTurn`/equivalent function.
examples: GOOD (Lego): import { ChessAI } from '../../entities/ChessAI.js'; const ai = new ChessAI({ rules, depth: 2 }); after human move: setTimeout(() => ai.playMove(board, 'black'), 350);
GOOD fallback: import { SimpleOpponent } from '../../entities/SimpleOpponent.js'; — random legal moves are still better than no opponent.
BAD: chess game where after white moves the board says 'BLACK to move' but never moves anything — the human has to click for both colors. This is the bug the wisdom rule was added to prevent.
Tic-tac-toe: build a small inline minimax (depth ≤9 is fast on the 3x3 board) for perfect play, or use SimpleOpponent for easy mode.

guidelines.rule: Board games with ELO or score tracking MUST submit final scores to the leaderboard using `window.mChatAI?.leaderboard?.submit({ score, metadata: { opponent, moves, duration_seconds } })`. Show a post-game rank card on the result screen. For chess/checkers/go: score is ELO delta or win=1/loss=0/draw=0.5 * 100.
guidelines.why: Board games have natural score moments (win/loss/ELO change). Wiring leaderboard at these moments costs 3 lines of code and converts a one-time play into a competitive experience.
examples: Chess win: leaderboard.submit({ score: 1, metadata: { opponent: 'AI-medium', moves: gameHistory.length } })
Rank card: 'You placed #3 this week with 12 wins!'
Show top 5 players table below the result overlay

guidelines.rule: If `window.mChatAI?.cloud?.isAvailable()` is true, offer a 'Strong AI' difficulty toggle that calls `window.mChatAI.cloud.llm()` for move selection instead of the built-in heuristic. Label it '⚡ Strong AI' with a token cost badge. Fall back to built-in AI if the call fails or is not available.
guidelines.why: Cloud AI opponents dramatically improve the game experience for serious players. The local AI (random/minimax-depth-3) can be beaten easily. Cloud LLM move selection provides human-level challenge without bloating the app bundle.
examples: Difficulty selector: [Easy] [Medium] [⚡ Strong AI (1 token/game)]

Cloud move: window.mChatAI.cloud.llm({ prompt: 'You are a chess grandmaster. Board FEN: ' + fen + '. What is the best move? Respond with only the move in algebraic notation.' }).then(move => makeMove(move.trim()))
Fallback: if cloud call fails or times out >3s, use local AI and show toast 'Using local AI'

guidelines.rule: Every game MUST present a VISIBLE on-screen restart/play-again button when the game ends (game-over, you-win, level-complete). Do NOT rely solely on a menu shortcut like Cmd+N or 'Game > New Game' — users don't discover those mid-play. The restart UI must: (a) appear automatically when the terminal phase is reached, (b) be visually distinct (colored button or large tappable area), (c) clearly say 'Restart' / 'Play Again' / 'New Game' (not just 'OK' or unlabeled), (d) when tapped, fully reset all game state (score, lives, level, score-multiplier, fuel, terrain — anything that accumulated during play). Keep the menu-bar shortcut too (e.g. Cmd+N), but the on-screen button is the primary affordance.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on shipped thrust-and-land: 'There is no restart button for the game: That should be a fundamental wisdom rule to all games if we don't have that already.' The shipped thrust-and-land rendered 'GAME OVER score=0' as static HUD text with no UI affordance. Users had to discover Cmd+N from the menu bar. Same pattern was hit by earlier shipped paddle-bounce, burger-stomp arcade, flap-and-joust — they all have the menu shortcut but lack a tappable on-screen restart. This is the single most common 'it's a great game but UX feels rough' complaint.
examples: RIGHT (SwiftUI overlay): When phase becomes .gameOver, render `VStack { Text('GAME OVER').font(.largeTitle); Text('Score: \(score)'); Button('Play Again') { restart() }.buttonStyle(.borderedProminent) }.padding(20).background(...).overlay(...)`
RIGHT (SpriteKit overlay): On game-over, add an SKShapeNode panel with 'Game Over' label + an SKLabelNode 'Tap to Restart' that the scene's touchesBegan/mouseDown handler responds to.
RIGHT (HTML game): On game-over, show a centered modal with 'Play Again' button. The modal is dismissable only by clicking the button (don't auto-dismiss).
WRONG: Showing 'GAME OVER score=0' in the HUD label and stopping the game loop. User can't restart without quitting and relaunching.
WRONG: Only Cmd+N / menu shortcut. Users don't think to look there mid-play.
Bonus: keyboard shortcut on the button itself (e.g. Space or Return = Play Again) for users who prefer keyboard.

guidelines.rule: Game backgrounds MUST be warm/atmospheric - radial-gradient or layered linear-gradient with at least one color highlight per corner. NOT pure black, NOT plain solid color. The web Candy Match reference uses a layered gradient: base purple (#1f1026) + radial yellow glow at top-left + radial cyan glow at top-right. Same pattern works for any game: pick a primary color matching the game's theme (purple for candy, dark blue for space, dark green for forest), add 1-2 radial corner glows in complementary colors. CSS: set the app root to `background: radial-gradient(circle at top left, rgba(255,220,90,0.35), transparent 45%), radial-gradient(circle at top right, rgba(90,220,255,0.30), transparent 45%), #1f1026;` and make it fill the viewport (min-height: 100vh). The warmth makes the game feel like a finished product instead of a wireframe.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'The black background is lame'. Switched to layered radial-gradient (purple base + yellow + cyan corners) per the web Candy Match reference. The atmosphere is the difference between a play-test prototype and a polished app — and it costs ~12 lines of SwiftUI to add. Apply broadly: arcade games, board games, card games, puzzles all benefit.
examples: RIGHT (SwiftUI): ZStack { Color(red: 0.12, green: 0.06, blue: 0.15).ignoresSafeArea(); RadialGradient(gradient: Gradient(colors: [Color.yellow.opacity(0.22), .clear]), center: UnitPoint(x: 0.2, y: 0.1), startRadius: 0, endRadius: 360); RadialGradient(...) }

RIGHT (HTML/CSS): background: radial-gradient(circle at 20% 10%, rgba(250,204,21,0.22), transparent 28rem), radial-gradient(circle at 80% 0%, rgba(34,211,238,0.16), transparent 26rem), linear-gradient(145deg, #170b1c 0%, #1f1026 48%, #35141d 100%);
WRONG: backgroundColor = .black, .ignoresSafeArea(), nothing else. Reads like a debug build.
Board game color picks: chess = warm beige + dark walnut accents; checkers = forest green felt + tan corner glow; poker = dark green felt + brass corner highlights.

guidelines.rule: Every clickable/interactive tile in a game (candy in match-3, card in solitaire, key on a piano keyboard, etc.) MUST have: (a) a vertical or 145deg linear-gradient fill, NOT a flat color. (b) An inner highlight - a rotated semi-transparent white blob or arc, inset ~12% from the edges, that gives the tile a 3D / glossy appearance. (c) A subtle 1.5px white-with-low-opacity stroke for definition. (d) A drop-shadow whose color is the tile's BOTTOM gradient color so the tile feels grounded. CSS: `.tile { background: linear-gradient(145deg, var(--tile-top), var(--tile-bottom)); border: 1.5px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.28); box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.4); position: relative }` plus an inner shine via `.tile::before { content: ''; position: absolute; inset: 12%; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.22); transform: rotate(18deg); border-radius: 40% }`.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'The jewels look kind of lame, the graphics are very flat and boring.' Adding the gradient + inner shine + colored shadow makes a flat shape look like a candy-shop jewel without bundling any art assets. Same trick works for cards (gradient front + back), keys (white-to-cream gradient + shine), pieces (color-toned gradient + shadow).
examples: RIGHT (Match3 candy): see /Users/lawrencenorman/mystuff/src/mchatai_platform/mchatai-source/frameworks/macos-components/examples/match3/ContentView.swift (function candyTileBody) — gradient + inner shine + stroke + colored shadow
RIGHT (web Candy Match): see frameworks/web-components/examples/candy-match/candy-match.css — .tile + .tile::before + .tile.kind-N gradient classes
WRONG: RoundedRectangle.fill(Color.red) + Image(systemName: 'sparkle') with no gradient, no shine, no shadow — reads as a wireframe placeholder
Reskin guidance: when the user asks for a different theme (e.g. 'space-themed match-3'), keep the gradient+shine+stroke STRUCTURE, only swap the gradient color pairs.

guidelines.rule: Every game state transition that the player triggers MUST have a visible animation, not a snap-cut. Specifically: (1) Tile/piece moves: animate `transform: translate(...)` with `transition: transform 0.25s cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1)` (overshoot easing gives a spring feel). (2) Tile-swap (match-3, sliding puzzles): both tiles slide simultaneously toward each other via transform, then settle. (3) Tile/piece removal: scale to 1.3x + fade-out (`transition: transform 0.18s ease-out, opacity 0.18s ease-out`). (4) Refill / collapse (match-3, tetris): new tiles slide DOWN from above via a keyframe (`@keyframes drop { from { transform: translateY(-120%); opacity: 0 } to { transform: translateY(0); opacity: 1 } }`), or use the `effects.spawn-drop` Lego component. (5) Cascade chain: stagger by 0.20-0.25s per chain pass using `transition-delay` (or `animation-delay`) so the player can see each step. (6) Score increment: a brief scale-pulse on the score number via a keyframe, or use the `effects.score-rise` Lego component. NEVER use snap-cuts on user-initiated state changes - even 0.1s of motion is the difference between 'game' and 'spreadsheet'.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'the animations are still pretty lame, the jewels don't slide down, there is no pulsing hints, nothing.' Borrowing the web Candy Match's animation choreography (swap glide + pop+fade match + slide-from-top refill) made the game feel alive instead of teleporty. Apply broadly: chess piece moves should glide, card flips should rotate, hop-games should arc, everything you click should respond visibly.
examples: RIGHT (swap glide): withAnimation(.easeInOut(duration: 0.18)) { tileOffsets[a] = ...; tileOffsets[b] = ... }; DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.20) { engine.swap(a, b); tileOffsets[a] = .zero; tileOffsets[b] = .zero }
RIGHT (pop fade): withAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.20)) { poppingTiles.formUnion(matched); ... } -> tile body uses .scaleEffect(isPopping ? 1.3 : 1.0).opacity(isPopping ? 0 : 1)
RIGHT (cascade stagger): for (i, event) in events.enumerated() { DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + Double(i) * 0.24) { ... } }
WRONG: engine.swap(a, b); engine.findMatches(); engine.collapse(); engine.refill() — all in one synchronous frame; the user sees the board jump from old state to new state with zero feedback.
Anti-pattern: rendering NEW tiles in their final position. They should slide IN from off-screen / above. Use transitions: .move(edge: .top).combined(with: .opacity).

guidelines.rule: After ~5 seconds of player idleness in a game with valid-move gameplay (match-3, sudoku, mahjong-tile-matching, etc.), the game MUST surface a hint by visibly pulsing one valid move. The hint should: (a) compute a real valid move via the engine's findPossibleSwap / findPossibleMove API, (b) animate the involved tiles with a soft scale (1.05x) + glow pulse on a 1s ease-in-out cycle, (c) cancel the hint as soon as the player makes any move. This is the difference between 'I'm stuck' and 'this game wants me to figure it out alone.' Cap idle threshold at 5-8s — any longer feels like punishment.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'no pulsing hints, nothing'. The web Candy Match doesn't have this either, but it's a UX standard for tile-swap puzzlers. Added to Match3 with a Timer.publish(every: 1) ticker + lastMoveAt tracking + findPossibleSwap helper. Same pattern works for Mahjong (highlight a matchable pair), Sudoku (highlight a cell with only one valid digit), Solitaire (highlight a moveable card).
examples: RIGHT (idle threshold + scale-pulse): @State var lastMoveAt = Date(); Timer.publish(every: 1) { if Date.now - lastMoveAt > 5 { hintPair = engine.findPossibleSwap(); withAnimation(...) { hintPulse.toggle() } } }
RIGHT (cancel on move): when the player commits any swap/move, set hintPair = nil; lastMoveAt = Date()
WRONG: showing the hint immediately after every move (turns it into a tutorial mode the player can't disable)
WRONG: never showing a hint (advanced players don't need it, but new players quit when they're stuck and don't realize valid moves exist)

guidelines.rule: Falling tiles in tile-based games (match-3, falling-block puzzle, sliding-tile, match-three puzzle, Threes) MUST deform: stretch vertically (sx≈0.92, sy≈1.12) while falling, squash horizontally (sx≈1.18, sy≈0.86) for ~130ms when they land. This is principle #1 of the 12 Disney principles (squash-and-stretch) — the difference between 'graphic moves' and 'candy lands.' Tiles that scale uniformly or just translate feel weightless and stickered onto the board. The squash is volume-preserving (sx*sy≈1.0).
guidelines.why: Real match-three puzzle production animations validated 2026-05-04 via web research + Match3 catalog reference rebuild (v6). Without squash-stretch the user said 'they feel like pngs that are just being animated.' With it the tiles read as having weight.
examples: RIGHT: phaseScales(.falling) returns (0.92, 1.12); phaseScales(.squashing) returns (1.18, 0.86); driven by AnimationIntensity.fallStretch and .squashAmount.
RIGHT (with AnimationIntensity): use the `effects.animation-intensity` Lego component — one struct field controls both stretch and squash, presets handle game pace.
WRONG: tiles use a uniform scale (1.0,1.0) the entire fall; reads as weightless icons sliding.

guidelines.rule: Match clears (or any 'item disappears with feedback' moment) MUST bulge before they shrink — anticipation (12 principles #4) + follow-through (#5). Sequence: (a) scale up 1.20–1.30× over 60–100ms with easeOut, (b) shrink to 0 + fade with easeIn over 80–110ms. A flat fade (just opacity 1→0) feels mechanical; bulge-shrink feels satisfying. The eye registers 'something is about to happen' (bulge) BEFORE the disappearance (shrink). The whole motion takes ~180ms.
guidelines.why: v3/v4/v5 of the catalog Match3 used flat fades and the user reported 'animations are lacking, feels flat.' v6 with bulge-shrink read as 'satisfying pop.' Universal animation principle — applies to card flips, item pickups, balloon pops, any binary disappear event.
examples: RIGHT: withAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.08)) { phase = .bulging }; sleep 80ms; withAnimation(.easeIn(duration: 0.10)) { phase = .shrinking }; commit clear after shrink.
RIGHT (with AnimationIntensity): bulgeAmount=0.25, bulgeDuration=0.08, shrinkDuration=0.11 are the standard preset.
WRONG: withAnimation(.easeOut) { tile.opacity = 0; tile.scale = 0 }  — single-phase fade-shrink, no anticipation, feels mechanical.

guidelines.rule: Tile-based games MUST use DIFFERENT animation curves for different phases. Pop/bulge = a fast ease-out (CSS cubic-bezier(0.2, 0.8, 0.2, 1), ~80ms). Shrink/clear = ease-in (~100ms, accelerate as it disappears). Land/drop = an overshoot 'spring' feel (cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1), ~180ms). Idle restore = a gentle settle (~200ms ease-out). One global transition curve for everything reads as 'boring ease on everything' - different timings per phase is what separates 'alive' from 'sterile'.
guidelines.why: Real match-three puzzle uses different curves per phase (researched 2026-05-04). v3/v4/v5 of catalog Match3 used one global spring; user feedback was 'feels flat.' v6 with per-phase curves was the first one that 'felt like candy.' Universal — applies to any squash-stretch animated UI.
examples: RIGHT: animationFor(.bulging) returns .easeOut(0.08); animationFor(.shrinking) returns .easeIn(0.10); animationFor(.squashing) returns .spring(response:0.18, dampingFraction:0.45). Apply each via .animation(animationFor(phase), value: phase) on the tile.
WRONG: withAnimation(.spring) wrapping the entire match-resolve cycle — every phase rides one curve, feels uniform.

guidelines.rule: Centralize the squash/bulge/spring magnitudes behind ONE intensity scale (a CSS custom property like --anim-intensity, or a single JS constant) instead of hardcoding magic numbers per effect. Pick a level by game pace: subtle for productivity-flavored puzzles (crossword/word/sudoku/utility), standard for mechanics-driven games (match-3/minesweeper/breakout), punchy for arcade-action (asteroids/pacman/kids-games). One value rescales every effect together; individual effects can override. Comment the choice (/* anim-intensity: subtle */) so it is scannable.
guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, tuning means hunting for magic numbers across the View; copying numbers from one game to another forgets to scale them for pace. AnimationIntensity centralizes the tuning surface. Filed 2026-05-04 after Match3 v6 work + user request 'might almost be nice to define controls that allow us to dial up and down some of this stuff so we can quickly tune a given game or app.'
examples: RIGHT: `private let intensity: AnimationIntensity = .standard  // AnimationIntensity: standard (match-3 = mechanics-driven feedback)` then use `intensity.bulgeAmount`, `intensity.flashScale`, etc. throughout the View.
RIGHT (override individual): `var i = AnimationIntensity.standard; i.flashScale = 0.5; i.flashOpacity = 0.4 // dial flashes back further` and assign to `intensity`.
WRONG: hardcoded `.scaleEffect(1.25)`, `.font(.system(size: 92))`, `.spring(response: 0.18, dampingFraction: 0.45)` scattered across the View — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Per-event flash overlays (sparkle for 3-match, burst for 4-match, lightning for 5-match — and any equivalent 'event happened here' visual) should be SCALED DOWN and SEMI-TRANSPARENT, not full-size solid graphics. Rule of thumb: glyph size ~60% of cell size, opacity 0.4–0.6, halo radius modest. Overly large/solid flash overlays read as 'sticker pasted on top of the gameplay' rather than 'effect emanating from the gameplay.' Real production overlays are accents, not centerpieces. Avoid giant text labels like '⚡ 5-MATCH ⚡' — the game-mechanic feedback (score increment, board change) is the centerpiece; the flash is punctuation.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on catalog Match3 v6.3: 'the animation with the star and the lightning bolt are a bit too big and should probably be a little more transparent.' v6.4 flashes use scale=0.6 + opacity=0.55 + halo=18, removed text labels — and the flashes still register but no longer dominate. AnimationIntensity captures this as `flashScale: 0.6, flashOpacity: 0.55` in the standard preset.
examples: RIGHT: Image(systemName: "sparkles").font(.system(size: 56 * intensity.flashScale, weight: .black)).foregroundStyle(color.opacity(intensity.flashOpacity)).shadow(color: color.opacity(intensity.flashOpacity * 0.8), radius: intensity.flashHaloRadius)
RIGHT: NO 'YOU DID IT' / '5-MATCH' text labels — the score increment + board change communicates the event.
WRONG: opaque 96pt bolt.fill + 150pt circle + 60pt halo + giant Text('⚡ 5-MATCH ⚡') label centered on top of the board. Reads as 'graphic asset' not 'effect.'

guidelines.rule: Game-flavor web mini-apps (arcade, match-3, card, puzzle, board games) SHOULD use the resources.audio-manager Lego component for sound instead of hand-rolling new Audio() / AudioContext plumbing. AudioManager provides pooled low-latency playback, a debounce guard, music fade in/out, a persisted mute (localStorage), and a semantic SFX dispatcher (audio.sfx('match'), 'hit', 'gameover', 'levelup', 'click'). It is iOS-Safari-safe (defers the first AudioContext.resume() to a user gesture). Pair with ui.mute-toggle, or apply the recipe.with-sound-effects addon. Do not double-wire: if the base game already has its own audio, stop and report 'already-sonified'.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after building SoundEngine component for catalog Match3 v7. User feedback: 'sound effects really add to the overall game experience.' Without a shared engine, every game reinvents pool management, debouncing, mute toggle, music fades — and gets 2-3 of them wrong (jarring volumes, no fade, plays on every render).
examples: RIGHT: import effects.sound-engine; @ObservedObject private var sound = SoundEngine.shared; on match-clear: sound.play(.match3Pop, pitchSemitones: cascadeDepth * 2)
RIGHT (mute button): Button { sound.muted.toggle() } label: { Image(systemName: sound.muted ? "speaker.slash.fill" : "speaker.wave.2.fill") } — required by audio-always-mute-toggle rule.
WRONG: build AVAudioPlayer pool ad-hoc in ContentView; reinvent debouncing; forget mute persistence; skip music fades.

guidelines.rule: Any game with sound MUST expose a visible, persistent mute toggle in the always-visible UI (not hidden in a settings menu). Default state: unmuted at modest volume (intensity.standard.sfxVolume = 0.65). Mute state MUST persist across app restarts via UserDefaults. Use SF Symbols `speaker.wave.2.fill` (unmuted) / `speaker.slash.fill` (muted). Optional but recommended: bind to keyboard shortcut 'M'. SoundEngine.muted handles the persistence — just observe `sound.muted` for the icon swap.
guidelines.why: Universal UX rule. Users tab between games, take calls, share screens. Burying mute deep in a settings menu means the next time they alt-tab in, the SFX surprise everyone in the room. Filed 2026-05-04 alongside SoundEngine ship — incorporated as default behavior in the engine itself but the visible-button is a View-level requirement that can't be enforced by the Lego.
examples: RIGHT: speaker icon in the bottom controls bar, always visible. Tap → mute persists.
RIGHT: keyboardShortcut("m", modifiers: []) for power-users + .help() tooltip.
WRONG: mute hidden in a 'Preferences' menu / hamburger / kebab. Surfacing-cost too high.

guidelines.rule: For match-3 / cascade / chain-reaction games, pitch-shift the SFX up per cascade depth (depth 0 = neutral, depth 1 = +2 semitones, depth 2 = +4, etc.) for the satisfying ascending arpeggio match-three is famous for. With resources.audio-manager / Web Audio, raise pitch by setting the source playbackRate to 2 ** (semitones/12) (e.g. depth*2 semitones). Cap at depth 6 (+12 semitones = double pitch); beyond that it sounds chipmunky. Apply the same pattern to combo counters, enemy streak-kills, and multi-line-clear in falling-block puzzle.
guidelines.why: Real match-three puzzle production behavior validated 2026-05-04 via web research + Match3 v7 implementation. Without pitch-shift, every cascade pop sounds identical → user can't tell they're chaining. With pitch-shift, the ear hears 'thing 1, thing 2, thing 3' as ascending tones and the cascade feels satisfying. Universal — applies to any game with chainable events.
examples: RIGHT: sound.play(.match3Pop, pitchSemitones: cascadeDepth * 2) — direct swap = neutral, first cascade = +2, second = +4, etc.
RIGHT (combos): sound.play(.arcadeExplosion, pitchSemitones: min(comboCount, 6) * 2)
WRONG: same flat tone on every cascade pop — user can't tell they're chaining.

guidelines.rule: Sound effects must fire on DISCRETE GAME EVENTS (match resolved, button tapped, enemy hit, level up), NOT inside a render/draw loop or reactive re-render. Web Audio resumes lazily on the first user gesture; debounce repeated SFX yourself (a ~30ms per-sound guard). If you call audio.sfx('x') from inside a requestAnimationFrame draw callback or a render that fires every frame, you hammer the engine and the SFX drift out of sync with what the user did. Place SFX calls inside the event handler where the thing happens (the click/keydown listener, or the engine step where matches resolve) - never the render.
guidelines.why: Same anti-pattern as 'wrote tile content into ForEach Text() body' — render-time side effects = unpredictable. Filed 2026-05-04 after wiring Match3: original instinct was to call sound.play(.match3Pop) inside the `flashView` ViewBuilder. That would have fired on every flash render, not just when the flash was created. Moved into the cascade-loop where events.kind is known once per resolution.
examples: RIGHT: in the cascade loop where match events resolve, exactly once per event: `for event in previewEvents { sound.play(matchSFX(for: event.kind), pitchSemitones: event.cascadeDepth * 2) }`
WRONG: `var body: some View { Text("\(score)").onAppear { sound.play(.scoreTick) } }` — fires every render.

WRONG: `.onChange(of: engine.score) { sound.play(.coinPickup) }` triggers on EVERY score change including the +30 from a 3-match → could fire 6+ times in a single cascade. Either debounce upstream or fire on the discrete match event, not the score derivative.

guidelines.rule: Same pattern as AnimationIntensity: pick a SoundIntensity preset by game pace. `.subtle` (sfxVol=0.35, musicVol=0.15) for productivity-flavored puzzles where audio is light punctuation. `.standard` (0.65/0.35) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper). `.punchy` (0.85/0.55) for arcade-action (asteroids, pacman, plants-vs-zombies, kids/casual). `.soft` (0.5/0.45) for relaxing/idle/ambient (solitaire, pattern matchers). Stamp the choice in a comment near the SoundEngine declaration: `// SoundIntensity: standard (match-3 mechanics-driven feedback)`.
guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, generators copy-paste a volume float and forget to scale for pace. Match-3 at .punchy is too loud; arcade at .subtle disappears. Presets capture the calibration. Mirrors the AnimationIntensity pattern intentionally so generators learn one mental model.
examples: RIGHT: SoundEngine.shared.intensity = .standard  // SoundIntensity: standard (match-3)
RIGHT (override): var i = SoundIntensity.standard; i.sfxVolume = 0.5; i.musicVolume = 0.25; SoundEngine.shared.intensity = i
WRONG: hardcoded `sound.play(.match3Pop, volume: 0.7)` everywhere — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Background music tracks MUST fade in (1.0–1.5s) and fade out (1.0s) — NEVER hard-cut. Hard cuts are jarring and feel like a glitch. SoundEngine.loop(_:fadeIn:) handles this: `sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5)`. Track switches: stopMusic(fadeOut: 0.3) THEN loop(.next, fadeIn: 1.0) — for a clean crossfade-via-stop. App-quit / sheet-dismiss / scene-change: stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) before transition. SFX are exempt — they're already short, fade is built into the sound itself.
guidelines.why: Universal audio production rule. Hard cuts on long-form audio = listener notices the seam = breaks immersion. Filed 2026-05-04 with SoundEngine ship — the engine enforces fade defaults so hard cuts only happen if a generator explicitly passes 0.
examples: RIGHT: sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5) on game start; sound.stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) on game-over screen.
WRONG: musicPlayer.play() / musicPlayer.stop() without fade. Listener hears the abrupt cut.

guidelines.rule: Progression-flavored games (match-3, arcade-with-waves, puzzles-with-difficulty-tiers, runners, idle clickers, platformers with stages) SHOULD use the web level Lego components instead of inventing target-score / progression math from scratch: entities.level-content-data (data-driven campaign + DifficultyScaler + localStorage save/load; LevelContentData.generate({ count, baseTarget, scale }) builds a smooth curve), entities.level-progression (runtime state + cumulative score for the hosted-page leaderboard bridge), and ui.level-select-screen (the picker). Read scaled gameplay knobs via level.params.<knob> instead of hardcoding per-level. Or apply the recipe.with-level-progression addon, which composes all three.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager ship. Without a shared component, every progression game writes ad-hoc 'targetScore *= 1.5' arithmetic, forgets to persist current level, and has no celebration moment — users finish level 1, see the same screen, and lose engagement. LevelManager + the celebration window turn 'beat target' into a reward.
examples: RIGHT: @StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(gameID: "match3", baseTarget: 1500, baseMovesAllowed: 30, curve: .standard); HUD reads `Text("LEVEL \(levels.currentLevel)")` and `Text("\(levels.currentTarget)")`. On target hit: `levels.advance()`.
RIGHT (UI): scaleEffect on level pill = `1.0 + levels.celebrationProgress * 0.20`; level-up banner opacity = `Double(levels.celebrationProgress)`.
RIGHT (palette growth): `engine.symbols = Array(["A","B","C","D","E","F","G","H"].prefix(levels.suggestedPaletteSize))` — palette starts at 5 colors, grows by +1 every 5 levels, capped at 8.
WRONG: hardcoded `private let targetScore = 1500`, no progression. User beats it, sees nothing change, exits app, never returns.
WRONG: ad-hoc `if score >= 1500 { score = 0; targetScore = 2500 }` — no curve, no persistence, no celebration.

guidelines.rule: When a player advances a level, the UI MUST acknowledge it with a 1-3 second celebration: (a) a prominent 'LEVEL N' banner that inflates and fades, (b) a level-up SFX (SoundEngine.play(.levelUp) — pitch-shift +2 semitones for stronger effect), (c) a brief scale/glow pulse on the level pill / score readout, (d) a tactile pause (~0.5–1s) before the new board / wave is presented. Skipping the celebration means the player isn't told they accomplished something — and the dopamine hit of progression is the entire reason to play. LevelManager provides `celebratingLevelUp: Bool` and `celebrationProgress: CGFloat` (0→1→0 across the window) — bind animations to these.
guidelines.why: Universal game-design principle. Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Without celebration, a level-up feels like a level-down — the user hits the target and the game just changes the number. With celebration (banner + SFX + pulse), the user FEELS the win. Match3 v8 implementation: level pill scaleEffect(1.0 + celebrationProgress * 0.20), banner Text scaleEffect(0.7 + celebrationProgress * 0.6) + opacity(celebrationProgress), levelUp SFX with +2 pitch.
examples: RIGHT: levels.advance() triggers celebrationProgress ramp 0→1 over 0.5s, plateau 0.5s, 1→0 over 2s. View binds banner opacity/scale to celebrationProgress; SFX plays at the start.
RIGHT (audio sequence): on target hit → sound.play(.victory) → 450ms pause → sound.play(.levelUp, pitchSemitones: 2) → levels.advance() triggers banner.
WRONG: levels.advance() called silently with no UI reaction. Player isn't sure if anything happened.
WRONG: instant snap-cut to next level (no banner, no SFX, no delay). Feels like a glitch.

guidelines.rule: When a player fails a level (out of moves, lives depleted, time up), the game MUST allow replay of the SAME level — never auto-demote to a lower level or back to L1. Save the player's progression at all times. RESTART buttons should reset score/moves WITHIN the current level, not bump levels.currentLevel down. Players who hit a hard level will replay it many times; demoting them would be a punishment that destroys engagement. The only way to drop a level should be a deliberate user action (e.g. a 'Reset Progress' settings toggle, never the in-game RESTART button).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager. Hardcoded into LevelManager API: `replayCurrentLevel()` is a no-op on persistence; only `reset()` (explicit reset-progress action) resets currentLevel to 1. Universal UX rule — applied incorrectly, players quit the game forever after one rage-fail.
examples: RIGHT: RESTART button rebuilds engine, refreshes moves to levels.currentMovesAllowed, but does NOT touch levels.currentLevel.
RIGHT: 'Reset Progress' in settings menu calls levels.reset() — opt-in only.
WRONG: RESTART resets currentLevel to 1. Player who beat 5 levels and rage-quit on L6 has to redo all 5.

guidelines.rule: Higher levels should feel different across MULTIPLE dimensions, not just 'higher target number.' For tile/match games: grow the symbol palette (LevelManager.suggestedPaletteSize: 5→6 at L5, 7 at L10, 8 at L15), shrink moves budget if pacing demands it (.accelerating curve), bump animation intensity (LevelManager.suggestedAnimationIntensity: 'subtle' L1-4 → 'standard' L5-9 → 'punchy' L10+). For arcade games: spawn-rate (LevelManager.suggestedSpawnRateMultiplier: 1.0× → 3.0× capped at L20+), enemy variety, projectile speed, intermittent boss waves at L5/L10/L15. The TARGET getting larger is just one knob — alone, it produces 'same game, takes longer.' Multi-knob progression produces 'this level FEELS different.'
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Real match-three puzzle / match-three puzzle / etc. grow many things per level (board shape, special-tile generation rate, blockers, mission types). Reference apps in the catalog should at minimum grow palette + intensity for tile games, spawn-rate + intensity for arcade games. LevelManager exposes the suggested helpers so generators don't have to invent them.
examples: RIGHT (match-3): L1: 5 colors, .standard intensity. L5: 6 colors, .standard. L10: 7 colors, .punchy. L15: 8 colors, .punchy. Each level feels harder for a NEW reason.
RIGHT (arcade): L1: 2 enemies, slow projectiles, .standard intensity. L5: 4 enemies, faster projectiles, .standard. L10: 6 enemies + occasional bomb-spawn, .punchy.
WRONG: every level identical except target=basicTarget*1.4^N. Player perceives 'same game just slower to finish' — no surprise, no novelty, drops off.

guidelines.rule: Choose LevelCurve by intended play session length: `.casual` (1.5× per level, fast ramp) for short kid-friendly sessions where players should reach high levels in 10-15 minutes. `.standard` (1.4×) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper) where 30-60 min sessions are typical. `.hardcore` (1.3×, slow ramp) for endurance/idle/score-attack games designed for long-term engagement. `.accelerating` (gentle 1.3× early, climbs to 1.6× by L20+) for games that should onboard easily but become a real challenge — typical for puzzle franchises. `.custom` for games with non-monotonic difficulty (boss levels, breather levels, jumps).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Same generator-mental-model pattern as AnimationIntensity / SoundIntensity — pick a preset by game pace, not by inventing arithmetic. Wrong curve = engagement disaster: hardcore curve on a casual game = players quit at L4; casual curve on hardcore = players hit L99 in an hour and have nothing left.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "kid-game", curve: .casual) — fast progression, frequent level-up celebrations.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "match3", curve: .standard) — balanced.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "score-attack", curve: .hardcore) — slow ramp.
WRONG: pick a curve based on coin flip / didn't think about pace; ship a casual-curve match-3 (player ceiling-hits at L20) or hardcore-curve casual game (player gives up at L4).

guidelines.rule: Persist `currentLevel` (the structural progression) via UserDefaults. Do NOT persist `currentScore` (the within-level progress) — it should reset on app launch. Players don't expect to resume a partially-played level mid-game; they expect to come back, see their level number, and start that level fresh. LevelManager handles the level persistence under key 'LevelManager.<gameID>.currentLevel'. SoundEngine.muted is also persisted (separately). Anything else (current score, board state, current cascade depth, hint pair) is ephemeral.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager. Saving mid-level state is high-effort + low-value: requires Codable engine state, can desync if engine impl changes, players have to mentally context-switch back into a half-finished puzzle. Saving level-only is what users expect from casual-game UX.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager persists currentLevel automatically. App launches → onAppear sets engine.symbols based on suggestedPaletteSize, moves = currentMovesAllowed, status = 'Level N — target M'. Score starts at 0.
WRONG: persist current Match3Engine state across launches. Brittle, annoying for users, breaks when engine model evolves.

guidelines.rule: Personal best / high-score should be surfaced in the GAME-OVER (or run-summary) panel, NOT in the always-visible in-play HUD. During active play, in-play high-score chrome (BEST pills, live 'X to beat' / '+N ahead' deltas) is visual noise that distracts from the actual gameplay — players are mid-decision and don't want a constant comparison ticker. The end-of-run panel is when the comparison is meaningful: it's a single moment, the player has earned their score, and seeing 'Best: 12,450' alongside 'Your run: 11,200' creates the natural motivation to play again. Use HighScoreManager.commit(score:) at game-over → it returns true on a new best, fires celebrationProgress for a 'NEW BEST!' flourish on the panel. The in-play HUD shows ONLY the active counters (score / level / moves / time).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Initial implementation v9 put BEST + live delta + 'NEW BEST!' chrome in the always-visible HUD. User feedback: 'why did you go so overboard with the highscore thing? this is a super ugly header. have you seen a high score on a game, it shouldn't show up until the game is done so the user doesn't get so distracted.' Established games (match-three puzzle, match-three puzzle, Threes, every console arcade game) put high score in the game-over screen — never as constant in-play chrome. v9.1 moved it to a modal that appears only at moves==0.
examples: RIGHT: in-play HUD = SCORE | LEVEL | MOVES (just active counters). Game-over panel = OUT OF MOVES / NEW BEST! header, big run-total readout, 'Best: 12,450' line with trophy icon, PLAY AGAIN button.
RIGHT: highScores.commit(totalScore) at moves==0 OR game-end → returns true on new best → panel header flips to 'NEW BEST!' with gold styling.
WRONG: BEST pill always visible in the HUD with live 'N to beat' / '+N ahead' delta during play. Distracts from the gameplay decision.
WRONG: 'NEW BEST!' celebration that fires DURING play (e.g. mid-cascade). Save it for the game-over moment so it's a clean reward.

guidelines.rule: Multi-level games MUST track BOTH a cumulative score (carries across levels in the session) AND a per-level score (zeros each level for progress-toward-target). Surface BOTH in the HUD: TOTAL = cumulative session score (this is what you compare against personal best); LEVEL SCORE = this level's progress, drives the level-progress bar / star thresholds / level-up trigger. Wiring: `totalScore = baseTotalForLevel + engine.score`. On level-up: `baseTotalForLevel = totalScore` (anchor the new level's floor), then re-seed engine (engine.score back to 0). Without two-tier scores, players who beat 5 levels see 'their score' reset on level transitions and lose the sense of session progress.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after user feedback 'after going to a new level my score gets reset to 0 instead of setting some additional point goal.' Same conceptual error as level-replay-not-demote — UX should never feel like progress was wiped. Match-3 / RPG / arcade-with-waves / endless-mode all benefit. Two-score model is also what HighScoreManager.commit(score:) should be called with — the cumulative TOTAL, not the per-level score.
examples: RIGHT: HUD shows TOTAL: 12,450 prominently; LEVEL SCORE: 320 / 3,000 below it. On level-up, TOTAL stays at 12,450 + earnings; LEVEL SCORE flips to 0 / 4,200 for the new level.
RIGHT: highScores.commit(score: totalScore) — commits cumulative session total, not per-level.
WRONG: only show engine.score, which resets every level. User thinks they're starting over each time.
WRONG: commit per-level score to HighScoreManager — your 'best' becomes 'highest single level' instead of 'longest run.'

guidelines.rule: Mutable in-game counters (moves remaining, lives, ammo, time) MUST be guarded by `max(0, …)` and gated at their entry points. A `moves -= 1` followed by a refund-on-fail pattern can race or compound — multiple events firing close together can push the counter negative. Pattern: `guard moves > 0 else { /* show out-of-moves UI */; return }` BEFORE the action, then `moves = max(0, moves - 1)` only after the action commits successfully. Failed actions (bad swap / missed shot / etc.) should NOT charge the counter — players punished for mis-timing input churn out fast.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after user reported 'somehow I managed to get to -1 moves' in catalog Match3 v8. Bug was: `moves -= 1` ran before the swap-success check; refund only happened on the mis-input path. Fixed with explicit guard at entry + max(0,…) on the decrement + don't-charge-bad-swaps. Universal pattern for any decrementing counter.
examples: RIGHT: guard moves > 0 else { status = "Out of moves — RESTART to retry"; sound.play(.uiError); return }; ... if !didSwap { sound.play(.uiError); return /* don't charge */ }; moves = max(0, moves - 1)
WRONG: moves -= 1; if !didSwap { moves += 1; return }  — racy under rapid input, can bottom out below 0.
WRONG: charge a move on failed actions. Player who mis-drags loses count for nothing.

guidelines.rule: Game engines that run `resolveCascades()` / equivalent setup logic during init MUST explicitly zero the score at the end of init. Initial-fill cascades produce score increments that are NOT user-earned. Without explicit zeroing, fresh games + level-up board re-seeds start at 200-1000+ ghost score depending on seed luck. Pattern in init: `_ = resolveCascades(); lastResolvedMatches = []; score = 0`. Engine.swap() / engine.advance() — the user-driven entry points — are the only paths that should award score.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from real bug in catalog Match3Engine. init() called resolveCascades() to clear initial-fill matches before showing the board, which awarded score for each cascade. Only the event log was cleared, not the score. User report: 'my score never starts at 0.' Same pattern applies to falling-block puzzle (initial cleared rows during piece-spawn-warmup), RPG (XP from seed-creation enemies), any game with auto-resolve in init.
examples: RIGHT: init() { ... refillAll(); _ = resolveCascades(); lastResolvedMatches = []; score = 0 }
WRONG: init() runs resolveCascades() (which awards score), then only clears event log — fresh games start at 200-1000 'ghost' points.

guidelines.rule: Score readouts ≥1000 MUST use thousands separators ('12,450' not '12450'; '1,234,567' not '1234567'). Numeric readability drops sharply past 4 digits without separators. HighScoreManager.formatNumber(_:) provides the canonical formatter (uses NumberFormatter with .decimal style); use it for ALL score-like numbers (current score, best score, target, total, delta). Targets that are still 4-digit (like '1500') can be unformatted but consistency is better — format everything once you cross the threshold.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with HighScoreManager ship. Universal readability rule. '125000' is a wall of digits; '125,000' is instantly parseable. Costs nothing, consistency is high-value.
examples: RIGHT: Text(HighScoreManager.formatNumber(totalScore)) → '12,450'
RIGHT: Text("+\(HighScoreManager.formatNumber(delta)) ahead") → '+1,200 ahead'
WRONG: Text("\(totalScore)") → '12450' (no separator)

guidelines.rule: ANY square-grid board game (3x3 tic-tac-toe, 4x4 mastermind, 8x8 chess/checkers, 9x9 sudoku/minesweeper, 19x19 go, etc.) MUST keep cells AT 1:1 aspect ratio at every viewport width — including narrow phone widths and embedded iframe contexts where horizontal/vertical space can mismatch. **Mandatory CSS contract — do all four:** (1) BOTH `grid-template-columns: repeat(N, 1fr)` AND `grid-template-rows: repeat(N, 1fr)` on the board container. Forgetting `grid-template-rows` is THE most common bug — auto-sized rows stretch when the container has spare vertical space. (2) `aspect-ratio: 1 / 1` on the board container itself — locks the board to its width-derived height. (3) `width: 100%; max-width: min(95vw, 95vh, <DESIGN_MAX>px)` on the board so the board can't overshoot viewport height in portrait. (4) `width: 100%; height: 100%; min-width: 0; min-height: 0` on each cell so the grid sizes it; do NOT rely SOLELY on per-cell `aspect-ratio: 1` (it gets overridden when the parent grid auto-sizes rows). All four together is the only combination that produces equal squares deterministically across viewports.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-05: chess shipped with squished rectangles → fixed via the four-step contract. User feedback 2026-05-06 on shipped TTT: 'when I published it, I see that the grid squares can get squished and not be equal squares when the view gets compressed.' Same root cause — TTT had `aspect-ratio: 1` on board + `repeat(3, 1fr)` columns but NO `grid-template-rows: repeat(3, 1fr)`. When TTT was embedded in the publish frame (different aspect than wizard preview), columns took 1/3 of width but rows took 1/3 of `auto`-sized height. Cells became wider rectangles. The user explicitly asked the rule generalize 'to all square grid board games' — this version captures every member of that class (TTT, mastermind, sudoku, chess, checkers, etc.) and explicitly enumerates all four CSS knobs.
examples: RIGHT (TTT 3x3): .tt-board { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); grid-template-rows: repeat(3, 1fr); width: 100%; max-width: min(95vw, 95vh, 480px); aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; } .ttt-cell { width: 100%; height: 100%; min-width: 0; min-height: 0; }
RIGHT (chess 8x8): same pattern with repeat(8, 1fr) on rows + cols, max-width 720px.
RIGHT (sudoku 9x9, mastermind 4-pegs): same pattern with repeat(9, 1fr) or repeat(4, 1fr).

WRONG: .board { grid-template-columns: repeat(N, 1fr); aspect-ratio: 1; max-width: 480px; } .cell { aspect-ratio: 1; } — missing grid-template-rows AND missing width:100%;height:100%;min-* on cell. Cells squish whenever the publish frame has extra vertical space.
WRONG: only `aspect-ratio: 1` on the cell, no aspect-ratio on the board — board height stretches to fill parent, rows auto-size to row content not 1fr, cells go rectangular.

Verification (canary): after generation, run scripts/verify_miniapp.py + open in browser at multiple widths (320px, 412px, 768px, 1024px). Squares must remain visually 1:1 at every width. The publish frame (mchatai.com/hub/<id>/play) is a known stress case — embedded iframe with wide host width but constrained inner height.

guidelines.rule: In two-color piece games (chess, checkers, go, othello/reversi), the two teams MUST be visually distinguishable at a glance: one team near-white (#fdfcf6 or pure ivory), the other near-black (#0a0907 or true black). Use a contrasting OUTLINE/text-shadow (the OPPOSITE color, ~85% alpha) so each piece reads on BOTH light and dark squares. The outline trick: 4-direction 1px text-shadows in the contrast color give a CSS 'stroke' around the unicode glyph. Without an outline, white pieces vanish on light squares and black pieces vanish on dark squares. The user-facing test: scan the screen — can you tell at a glance which side is which without zooming in? If not, fail.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-05 on shipped chess: 'me the white player at the bottom of the screen should have white pieces, and the computer player (the black player should have black pieces).' v1 chess.css used `color: #111827` for black pieces with `text-shadow: 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,0.55)` — a single subtle white shadow that washed the dark glyph out on dark squares (#7a4b2d). Both teams visually read as 'whitish silhouettes.' Fixed by going to true-black/true-white fills + 4-direction contrast outlines so each piece carries its own outline regardless of square color.
examples: RIGHT (white piece): color: #fdfcf6; text-shadow: -1px -1px 0 #1a1208, 1px -1px 0 #1a1208, -1px 1px 0 #1a1208, 1px 1px 0 #1a1208, 0 6px 14px rgba(0,0,0,0.45);
WRONG: color: #111827 (off-black) + a single 1px white-low-alpha shadow — black pieces wash out on dark squares; color: #fffdf6 + a single dark shadow — white pieces wash out on light squares.

Unicode-glyph reminder: chess uses ♔♕♖♗♘♙ (white outlined glyphs) and ♚♛♜♝♞♟ (black filled glyphs) — these glyphs render as a single fill color, so it's the CSS color (and outline shadow) that distinguishes the two teams — never the unicode codepoint alone.

guidelines.rule: Two-player turn-based games (chess, checkers, othello) MUST anchor the human player at the BOTTOM of the screen and the AI/opponent at the TOP. Convention from physical board games: you sit on one side, opponent on the other, your pieces start closest to you. For chess specifically: human=white at the bottom (white starts on rank 1, which renders as the bottom row), AI=black at the top (black starts on rank 8, renders as the top row). Do NOT flip orientation, do NOT randomize. If the human chooses to play black, ROTATE the entire board so their pieces stay at the bottom — don't just give them black at the top. Render loop must iterate row 0 first (top) to row N-1 last (bottom) so DOM order matches visual order.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-05 on shipped chess: 'me the white player at the bottom of the screen should have white pieces.' Universal physical-board convention. Violating it forces the human to mentally rotate the board on every move, which is a non-trivial cognitive tax. Same rule applies to: checkers (red/black), othello (you=light, opp=dark), go (you=black per tradition or whichever you chose, opp=other), backgammon (your home quadrant at bottom). When humanColor != white in chess, the FIX is to flip the board, not flip the convention.
examples: RIGHT (chess, human=white): render rows 0..7 → black back-rank at top, white back-rank at bottom. Human clicks at bottom.
RIGHT (chess, human=black): render rows 7..0 → black back-rank at bottom (the human side), white at top. Algebraic coords still correct, just flipped.
WRONG: humanColor='black' but render still has black on top — human reaches across the whole board to move their own pieces.
WRONG: randomized rotation per game session. The user's spatial memory should be stable across plays.

guidelines.rule: Web-component extracts that may be inlined into single-file mini-apps MUST be self-contained — NEVER `extends` another component, and NEVER reference another component's symbol unguarded in a constructor or top-level statement. The single-file inline assembler (claude-cli, codex, etc.) extracts components verbatim and strips imports. If `entities.board8x8` extends `entities.grid-board`, and the LLM-driven assembler doesn't list grid-board as a selected starter component (or forgets to inline it), the resulting page hits `ReferenceError: GridBoard is not defined` at module-evaluation time → board never renders. Two fixes: (a) STRUCTURAL — inline the parent class's content directly into the child component (eliminate the `extends`); this is the canonical fix for tightly-coupled siblings like Board8x8/GridBoard. (b) DEFENSIVE — wrap optional helper class instantiation in a typeof guard: `(typeof AudioManager === "function") ? new AudioManager(...) : { stub }`. Apply (a) when the parent is small enough to inline (<200 lines). Apply (b) for optional helpers (audio, leaderboard, restart-overlay, mini-header).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 from chess canary 1: shipped index.html had `class Board8x8 extends GridBoard` but GridBoard was NEVER inlined (the LLM listed 8 of 11 starterComponents; grid-board, swatches, audio-manager dropped). ReferenceError → board empty. The recipe.chess `starterComponents` listed grid-board, but the LLM-driven assembly contract didn't enforce inlining the full set. Fixed by making Board8x8 self-contained (no extends) AND adding defensive guards in ChessGame for AudioManager/Leaderboard/RestartOverlay/MiniHeader. Same pattern applies to checkers (Board8x8 again), othello (Board8x8 + Reversi rules), any future board game leveraging shared infrastructure. Verify with `mchatai_macOS/scripts/verify_miniapp.py <id>` — flags `class X extends Y` where Y is undefined and ungguarded `new Z(...)`.
examples: RIGHT (structural — small parent inlined): export class Board8x8 { constructor(...) { /* all GridBoard methods inlined directly */ } isDarkSquare(row, col) {...} algebraicToCell(...) {...} cellToAlgebraic(...) {...} } — no extends, no transitive import.
RIGHT (defensive — optional helper): this.audio = (typeof AudioManager === "function") ? new AudioManager({...}) : { beep: () => {}, fadeIn: () => {}, fadeOut: () => {}, stop: () => {} };
RIGHT (defensive — optional UI mount): if (typeof MiniHeader !== "undefined" && MiniHeader && typeof MiniHeader.mount === "function") { MiniHeader.mount(host, opts); }
WRONG: export class Board8x8 extends GridBoard { ... } when the inline-assembler doesn't always carry GridBoard.
WRONG: this.audio = new AudioManager({...}); — direct call, throws if assembler dropped AudioManager.
Validation rule of thumb: `grep -c 'class .*extends [A-Z]' <installed>/index.html` — every match must have the parent defined as `class Parent` ALSO in the file.

guidelines.rule: Glue-code calls to swatch helpers (`applySwatchVariables`, `getSwatchByID` from `resources/Swatches.js`) MUST be guarded so a missing import doesn't crash the constructor and prevent the game from rendering. Pattern: `if (typeof applySwatchVariables === 'function') { const sw = resolveActiveSwatch(); if (sw) applySwatchVariables(document.documentElement, sw); }`. The synchronous `:root { --mchat-* }` fallback CSS block at the top of the stylesheet means the page is still styled even if JS swatch application is skipped. Crashing the constructor over a styling concern is the wrong tradeoff — the user gets an empty board instead of a styled-but-default board.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-05 on shipped chess: 'the board isn't showing up.' Root cause: the single-file inline assembler dropped the `import { applySwatchVariables, getSwatchByID } from '../../resources/Swatches.js'` line when collapsing module imports, but kept the `applySwatchVariables(document.documentElement, getSwatchByID('retro-neon'))` call in the ChessGame constructor. ReferenceError thrown → constructor never finished → `bindControls` / `reset` / `start` / `render` all skipped → board container stayed empty. Defensive guard means: assembler bug → visible-but-default-styled game; not assembler bug → swatch applied normally. Same pattern applies to any optional resource helper: AudioManager, Leaderboard, RestartOverlay — if the import gets dropped, guarded callers still render.
examples: RIGHT: if (typeof applySwatchVariables === 'function') { const sw = resolveActiveSwatch(); if (sw) applySwatchVariables(document.documentElement, sw); }

RIGHT (resolveActiveSwatch fallback chain): meta[name=mchat-swatch] (inline brief tokens) → meta[name=mchat-swatch-id] (named swatch) → getSwatchByID('retro-neon') default. So the visual director's brief drops in via `<meta>` injection without code changes.

WRONG: `applySwatchVariables(document.documentElement, getSwatchByID('retro-neon'))` directly in the constructor — if either symbol gets stripped by an inline assembler, the entire game fails to render.
WRONG: try/catch silently swallowing all errors in the constructor — hides real bugs. Only guard the helper calls; let other errors propagate.

guidelines.rule: When the AI Wizard's Visual Director (VD.7) stages a brief at `<wizard-tmp>/visual-brief/tokens.json`, the generated mini-app HTML MUST consume those tokens, not hardcode a swatch ID. Two patterns: (a) PREFERRED: emit a `<meta name="mchat-swatch" content='{"tokens":{...full token tree...}}'>` in `<head>` so the runtime applies them via `applySwatchVariables`; (b) ALTERNATIVE: emit a `<style>:root { --mchat-background: ...; --mchat-accent: ...; ... }</style>` block right after the existing fallback `:root { ... }` block (cascades win). Either pattern will be picked up by the resolveActiveSwatch() helper that reads meta first. Hardcoding `getSwatchByID('retro-neon')` IGNORES the user's chosen visual brief and ships a generic palette no matter what reference style was selected.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-05 on shipped chess: VD.7 selected nature-organic (sage green sage / cream / Cormorant Garamond serif), staged it correctly to `wizard-tmp/visual-brief/tokens.json`, but the installed mini-app still rendered in the retro-neon (cyan/orange/dark-blue) palette. Cause: the chess assembly source-of-truth had `applySwatchVariables(..., getSwatchByID('retro-neon'))` hardcoded. The VD brief never reached the page. Fix: source code reads meta override; generator/assembler injects meta from staged brief into HTML head. Without this, the entire VD.7 pipeline (mockup, evaluator, picker) is decoration that never reaches the user.
examples: RIGHT (assembler injects meta): <meta name="mchat-swatch" content='{"id":"nature-organic","tokens":{"background":"#F5F0E8","accent":"#B8763E",...}}'>
RIGHT (assembler injects style block): <style>:root{--mchat-background:#F5F0E8;--mchat-accent:#B8763E;--mchat-text:#2C2A23;...}</style>
RIGHT (source-of-truth code): const swatch = resolveActiveSwatch(); applySwatchVariables(document.documentElement, swatch);
WRONG: applySwatchVariables(document.documentElement, getSwatchByID('retro-neon')) — hardcoded; ignores VD brief.
Verification (canary): after a wizard run with a non-default brief, `grep mchat-swatch <installed>/index.html` — must find a meta or style override, not just the fallback :root block.

guidelines.rule: Game state transitions (level-up, game-over, round-restart, board reseed) MUST be SYNCHRONOUS and ATOMIC. Do NOT split a state mutation across setTimeout / await segments where the visual celebration runs in parallel with state-resetting code. While the user waits for timers they can tap/swap/fire, and the next frame reads HALF-MUTATED state - a cluster of bugs: score resets to 0 (new turn reads a stale baseline), counters do not refresh (UI bound to a value not yet reassigned), RESTART lands on the wrong level (level advanced but the engine was not swapped). Fix: do ALL state mutation in one synchronous step, and run the celebration banner via an independent timer / CSS animation that reads its own progress value. SFX is fine to fire on a delay - audio-only, no state mutation, cannot race.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from Match3 v9 → v9.2 incident. v9 split level-up across two `try await Task.sleep` segments (450ms + 650ms) with state-resets happening AFTER the sleeps. User reported three distinct bugs that were all the same race: 'my score went back to 0 at a certain level' (next cascade read stale baseTotalForLevel), 'moves didn't reset after some levels' (UI bound to moves var that hadn't been reassigned yet), 'RESTART only takes me back to the previous level' (RESTART read half-mutated state — level number advanced but engine not yet swapped). v9.2 made the entire transition atomic in one withAnimation block; banner animation runs from levels.celebrationProgress which advances on its own.
examples: RIGHT (atomic): when level target hit → run all in one turn: `levels.advance(); engine = newEngine; moves = levels.currentMovesAllowed; baseTotalForLevel = totalScore; victoryAnnounced = false`. The banner animates from levels.celebrationProgress (a @Published 0→1→0 value the controller advances internally). SFX fires as a one-shot Task — audio only.

RIGHT (visual delay decoupled from state): celebration banner reads `if levels.celebratingLevelUp { ... .opacity(Double(levels.celebrationProgress)) }`. Banner can take 3 seconds to fade out — gameplay state is already consistent at frame 1.
WRONG: `Task { try await Task.sleep(450ms); levels.advance(); try await Task.sleep(650ms); engine = newEngine; moves = newMoves }`. During the sleeps, user input lands on stale state.

WRONG: setting `victoryAnnounced = false` inside an async withAnimation that runs LATER than other state mutations — gives the next turn's runCascadeLoop() permission to re-fire the win condition before the engine has been swapped.
Acceptable async (audio only): `Task { try await Task.sleep(350ms); sound.play(.levelUp) }` — no state mutation, no race possible.

guidelines.rule: When building chess, checkers, or other classic board games, IMPORT the existing Rules + AI Lego from entities/ (ChessRules + ChessAI, CheckersRules + CheckersAI). Do NOT inline-implement legal-move generation, minimax, or piece-square-table eval. The shipped Lego already handles edge cases (king moves, en passant, captures) and integrates with MinimaxAI for alpha-beta search.
guidelines.why: Inline implementations consistently ship bugs the Lego already fixed. Examples we have seen: a chess artifact with a purely random move picker (no minimax at all); a checkers artifact that hard-coded forced-capture rules so the player could not decline a jump; both shipped before the Lego entities existed and have now been retrofitted by direct GCS patch. Each patch only fixes ONE published artifact. The fix MUST land in the Lego so the next prompt-generated game inherits it.
examples: import { ChessRules } from "./entities/ChessRules.js"; import { ChessAI } from "./entities/ChessAI.js";
import { CheckersRules } from "./entities/CheckersRules.js"; import { CheckersAI } from "./entities/CheckersAI.js";
BAD: writing your own getLegalMoves() / minimax() / evaluateBoard() inside the game module.

guidelines.rule: In checkers/draughts, captures are OPTIONAL by default. The player may decline a jump and play a normal move instead. CheckersRules.legalMoves() already returns both jump moves and simple moves together; do not filter out simple moves when a jump is available unless the user opted into tournament-strict mode.
guidelines.why: Forced captures frustrate casual players. The user explicitly asked for this: "forces jumping when player wants other moves. Should be optional unless tournament-strict." Defaulting to optional is friendlier and matches casual checkers conventions; tournament-strict is opt-in via a constructor option.
examples: new CheckersRules() // optional captures (default)
new CheckersRules({ forceCaptures: true }) // tournament-strict
Status text should read JUMP AVAILABLE when a jump exists, NOT JUMP REQUIRED.

guidelines.rule: ChessAI default search depth is 3, not 2. Depth 2 plays at a beginner level (often missing material threats); depth 3 plays competently and still moves in under 1 second on typical hardware. Pass { depth: 4 } only if you can afford ~3 second think times.
guidelines.why: A user reported the prior depth-2 default produced random-feeling moves: "I want a version of chess that is more competitive." Depth 3 with material + PST eval gives an opponent that defends pieces, threatens captures, and plays sensible openings.
examples: new ChessAI({ rules, depth: 3 }) // recommended default
new ChessAI({ rules, depth: 4 }) // strong, slow

guidelines.rule: When rendering Unicode chess/checkers/cards glyphs (\u2654-\u265F, suit symbols) on light-colored squares, apply a text-shadow such as text-shadow: 0 0 1px rgba(0,0,0,0.35), 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.30) to the cell or piece element. Hollow white-piece glyphs on light-tan board squares are nearly invisible without this.
guidelines.why: User reported "better B/W contrast" as a chess ask. Unicode chess outline-style white pieces (U+2654-U+2659) lack their own fill, so they read poorly on light squares without a stroke or shadow. Black pieces (U+265A-U+265F) are solid but still benefit slightly.
examples: .cell { text-shadow: 0 0 1px rgba(0,0,0,0.35), 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.30); }
Or apply text-stroke equivalent via SVG filter for sharper outlines.

guidelines.rule: Web mini-app board games have THREE valid composition modes. Pick by deploy target: (1) TRUE LEGO (import { Foo } from './web-components/.../Foo.js' + marker) for HUB-published games served by mchatai.com over HTTPS - works cleanly; (2) INLINED LEGO (paste canonical Lego components into one <script type='module'> with '// === inlined <id> ===' separators + marker) for LOCAL preview (file://) and as a safer default - zero runtime fetches but you still benefit from tested component code; (3) MONOLITH (single inline <script>, no marker, fully bespoke - see assets/scaffolds/board-game-monolith.html) only when the game has no shared primitive available (e.g. physics-canvas slingshots, bespoke arcade like sunset-slingshot). Verified examples in the wild: cribbage + word-quest = TRUE LEGO; spades = INLINED LEGO; sunset-slingshot + tiny-flappy-bird + texas-holdem-poker + candy-match = MONOLITH. Do NOT default to monolith just because the local Lego bundler had transitive-dep bugs - those are install-time bugs to fix, not signals that Lego is wrong.
guidelines.why: An earlier rule (now removed) blanket-recommended monolith because chess/kanban/beat-gate Lego attempts shipped black screens at local install. Investigation found those failures were install-bundler bugs (missing transitive deps like ChessAI -> MinimaxAI, JSON-string-encoded literal \n in kanban, etc.), not fundamental Lego problems. Real published Lego artifacts (cribbage, word-quest) render fine because the hub publish pipeline bundles web-components/ next to the artifact and serves over HTTPS. Inlined-Lego (spades) is a low-risk middle ground that sidesteps file:// scheme issues without rewriting game logic from scratch.

guidelines.rule: If you render chess pieces as Unicode glyphs colored via CSS (the common `const GLYPH = { pawn:'♟', ... }` + `.piece.white{color:#fff}` approach), append the U+FE0E text-presentation selector to the PAWN glyph — in JS write '♟\uFE0E'. U+265F (♟) is the ONLY chess codepoint with default emoji presentation, so on iOS/Safari it renders as a COLOR EMOJI that IGNORES your CSS color: the white pawns show up dark while ♔♕♖♗♘ and ♚♛♜♝♞ all render correctly. Simplest safe fix: append \uFE0E to EVERY glyph in the map (harmless for the non-emoji pieces) and/or set `font-variant-emoji:text` on the piece element. Invisible on the desktop/macOS preview — only breaks on a phone, so test glyph color on iOS.
guidelines.why: Verified 2026-07-01: a chess mini-app's white pawns rendered black on iOS while every other white piece was correct, because only U+265F has emoji presentation and iOS drew it as a color emoji ignoring .piece.white{color}.

guidelines.rule: Turn advancement must have EXACTLY ONE chokepoint. If a shared helper (e.g. commitMove/afterAction) already runs the end-of-action accounting, the click handler / AI driver must NOT call it again. Before shipping, trace one full move and count turn-advance calls: exactly one per action.
guidelines.why: A double advance is invisible in code review and in a render screenshot: the game LOOKS fine but the unwinding call stack consumes the HUMAN's next turn. Real bug 2026-07-12 (chaos-chess): player made 1 move, the CPU then played 3 in a row every cycle - 'I move once and get swarmed'.
examples: commitMove tail calls afterMarchAction -> initiateMove must ONLY call commitMove, never afterMarchAction too
Verify: subscribe to the turn manager's turnchange event, make one move, assert exactly one player->opponent transition

guidelines.rule: Any per-turn quota read by indexing a sequence with a phase counter (e.g. SEQUENCE[cycleStep]) must clamp or modulo the index, and interlude/special phases must early-return from normal-turn accounting instead of running with an out-of-range counter.
guidelines.why: When the counter sits past the array during an interlude, the quota is undefined: 'actions >= undefined' is never true so the turn can never auto-end, and prompts render '(NaN left)'. Combined with phase-guarded buttons this permanently wedges the player. Real bug 2026-07-12 (chaos-chess).
examples: const movesAllowed = () => SEQ[Math.min(step, SEQ.length - 1)]
function afterAction(color) { if (interlude) return; ... }

guidelines.rule: Whenever the UI prompts the player to act, at least one actionable element must exist (selectable piece, enabled button) OR an always-visible pass/skip control. Verify by SIMULATION (drive clicks in the running game), not by reading the code: 'prompted but nothing actionable' is a state-machine property, invisible statically.
guidelines.why: Players report this as 'I'm stuck here!' - the single worst playability failure. It survives render gates (the board renders fine) and code review (each branch looks correct in isolation).
examples: March prompt active -> at least one piece glows OR the END MARCH button is visible and enabled
Auto-pass the phase when no legal action exists, with a log line saying so

guidelines.rule: Expose a debug surface on globalThis named after the game (e.g. globalThis.myGame) with read-only getters for core state (board, turn manager, phase counters, gameOver) and the event-emitting turn manager itself. Do not gate it behind DEBUG flags; it is inert for players.
guidelines.why: Automated deep-play probes subscribe to turnchange/action events and read counters to detect turn-stealing, wedges and quota bugs in seconds. Without the surface, probes can only guess selectors and read pixels. The 2026-07-12 chaos-chess turn-stealing bug was root-caused in one probe run purely because the game exposed globalThis.chaosChess.
examples: globalThis.chaosChess = { get board(){return board}, get manager(){return manager}, get cycleStep(){return cycleStep}, get gameOver(){return gameOver} }
